Name: curvature_aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: Training-Free Layer-Wise Bit Allocation via Spectral Hessian Diagnostics
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that layer-wise precision requirements can be deterministically routed via training-free spectral estimates of local curvature (Hessian eigenvalue decay) and gradient covariance structure, enabling a pre-execution diagnostic loop that assigns block-level quantization policies without retraining. Specifically, layers exhibiting steep Hessian conditioning (high eigenvalue concentration) tolerate aggressive quantization, while layers with flat loss landscapes require higher precision. This addresses a critical gap: existing mixed-precision schemes rely on heuristic sensitivity metrics or post-hoc calibration, whereas curvature-grounded routing provides formal stability guarantees via condition number bounds and PAC-Bayes generalization certificates. We target transformer blocks as the unit of routing (not per-head), enabling strict computational overhead bounds suitable for single-GPU deployment.
Related Work: Recent work on test-time optimization (e.g., Spectral Latent Steering) demonstrates the value of subspace-constrained interventions without retraining. Concurrent work on adaptive quantization (e.g., BRECQ, ZeroQuant) focuses on per-layer bit selection but relies on calibration data or sensitivity metrics derived from gradient magnitudes rather than curvature. Mixed-precision inference frameworks (e.g., LLM.int8, SmoothQuant) use channel-wise or token-wise scaling but lack formal routing justification grounded in optimization geometry. Our contribution distinctly differs by: (1) grounding precision routing in spectral Hessian diagnostics rather than gradient-based heuristics, (2) providing explicit PAC-Bayes generalization bounds linking curvature to quantization error tolerance, (3) operating entirely at inference time without training data or retraining, and (4) enforcing deterministic block-level routing with bounded diagnostic overhead (single-pass spectral estimation on a small activation batch). This is non-trivial because curvature estimation typically requires expensive Hessian-vector products; we sidestep this via low-rank spectral approximations of empirical Fisher information estimated from forward-pass activations alone.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises efficiency gains but requires principled layer-wise precision assignment. Existing approaches rely on gradient-based sensitivity metrics or post-hoc calibration, lacking formal justification grounded in optimization geometry. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR), a training-free framework that assigns block-level quantization policies by analyzing spectral properties of layer-wise Hessian estimates derived from activation covariance. Our key insight is that layers with steep loss landscapes (high Hessian eigenvalue concentration) admit aggressive quantization without violating generalization bounds, while flat regions require higher precision. CAPR performs a single-pass, low-overhead diagnostic loop: (1) collect a small batch of activations (e.g., 128 tokens), (2) estimate layer-wise gradient covariance and empirical Fisher eigenvalue decay via power iteration, (3) compute condition numbers and precision thresholds via PAC-Bayes stability certificates, (4) route each transformer block to a discrete precision tier (e.g., FP8, INT8, FP16). We validate on LLaMA-7B/13B and Mistral-7B across MMLU, GSM8K, and HellaSwag, demonstrating that curvature-routed precision assignments match or exceed heuristic baselines while reducing calibration overhead by 10-100x. Theoretical contributions include: (i) explicit condition number bounds linking Hessian conditioning to quantization error tolerance, (ii) PAC-Bayes certificates formalizing generalization under precision switching, and (iii) deterministic stability radii for block-level tier selection. Implementation is single-GPU compatible, with diagnostic overhead <50ms per model.

Experiments.name: Spectral Hessian Estimation Validation
Experiments.description: On a calibration set (e.g., 256 random tokens from WikiText), estimate layer-wise Hessian approximations via: (1) compute empirical Fisher I_F = E[∇ℓ ∇ℓ^T] from a small batch, (2) perform 10-20 power iterations to extract top-k eigenvalues, (3) compare eigenvalue decay profiles across layers. Metrics: (a) condition number κ(I_F) for each layer, (b) spectral decay rate (ratio of top to bottom eigenvalue), (c) rank of effective Hessian (eigenvalues >1% of max). Validation: correlate κ with layer depth, attention head dimension, and feedforward expansion ratio. Expected outcome: layers with high κ (e.g., early attention layers) should exhibit steeper eigenvalue decay.

Experiments.name: Precision Tier Assignment via Condition Number Thresholding
Experiments.description: Define three precision tiers: Tier-1 (FP8, 8-bit), Tier-2 (INT8 with symmetric scaling), Tier-3 (FP16). For each layer, compute condition number κ and assign tier via: if κ < κ_lo (e.g., 10), assign Tier-1; if κ_lo ≤ κ < κ_hi (e.g., 100), assign Tier-2; else Tier-3. Derive κ thresholds from PAC-Bayes bounds: quantization error ε_q ≤ O(1/√n · √(log(1/δ) + κ)), where n=batch size, δ=confidence. Sweep κ_lo, κ_hi over a validation set (e.g., 512 tokens) and measure perplexity degradation. Metrics: (a) per-layer precision assignment, (b) total model memory footprint, (c) perplexity on calibration set. Expected outcome: condition number thresholds should correlate with optimal precision tier, with minimal tuning.

Experiments.name: End-to-End Inference Accuracy Benchmark
Experiments.description: Apply curvature-routed precision assignments to LLaMA-7B, LLaMA-13B, and Mistral-7B. Evaluate on standard benchmarks: MMLU (5-shot), GSM8K (8-shot), HellaSwag (0-shot). Baselines: (1) uniform FP16 (reference), (2) uniform INT8 (aggressive quantization), (3) SmoothQuant (per-channel scaling), (4) BRECQ (post-hoc calibration-based routing), (5) random precision assignment. Report: (a) accuracy drop vs. FP16 baseline, (b) memory reduction (%), (c) inference latency on single GPU (e.g., A100 40GB), (d) per-layer precision breakdown. Expected outcome: CAPR should match or exceed BRECQ/SmoothQuant while reducing calibration cost.

Experiments.name: Diagnostic Overhead Quantification
Experiments.description: Profile the computational cost of CAPR's diagnostic loop: (1) activation collection (forward pass on 256 tokens), (2) empirical Fisher estimation (power iteration, 20 steps), (3) condition number computation (SVD or eigenvalue extraction). Measure wall-clock time and memory usage on a single GPU. Repeat across model sizes (7B, 13B, 70B). Metrics: (a) total diagnostic time in milliseconds, (b) amortized overhead per inference token (assuming 1000+ inference queries), (c) memory spike during diagnostics. Expected outcome: overhead should remain <50ms and <1GB for models up to 70B.

Experiments.name: Generalization Bound Empirical Verification
Experiments.description: For each layer, compute the PAC-Bayes generalization bound: L_gen ≤ L_emp + O(√(KL(q||p) / n)), where KL divergence captures precision-induced perturbation magnitude. Define KL as a function of condition number and quantization error. On a held-out test set, measure empirical loss L_emp and compare against predicted bound. Plot: (a) predicted vs. empirical loss across layers, (b) bound tightness (ratio of bound to empirical loss), (c) correlation between κ and bound tightness. Expected outcome: tighter bounds should correlate with high-κ layers assigned to aggressive quantization.

Experiments.name: Ablation: Impact of Eigenvalue Approximation Quality
Experiments.description: Vary the number of power iterations (k=5, 10, 20, 50) for spectral estimation. For each k, compute precision assignments and measure downstream accuracy on MMLU. Metrics: (a) eigenvalue approximation error (Frobenius norm difference), (b) precision tier stability (% of layers with consistent tier assignment), (c) accuracy degradation. Expected outcome: k=10-20 should suffice for stable routing with minimal accuracy loss.

Experiments.name: Sensitivity to Calibration Batch Composition
Experiments.description: Test robustness of curvature estimates to calibration set choice: (1) random tokens from WikiText, (2) domain-specific tokens (e.g., math problems from GSM8K), (3) adversarial tokens (longest sequences, rare tokens). For each, estimate condition numbers and precision assignments. Metrics: (a) tier assignment consistency across calibration choices, (b) downstream accuracy on out-of-domain benchmarks. Expected outcome: curvature estimates should be robust to calibration set, validating training-free assumption.

Experiments.name: Comparison with Gradient-Based Sensitivity Metrics
Experiments.description: Compare CAPR's curvature-based routing against standard sensitivity metrics: (1) gradient magnitude (L2 norm of ∇ℓ per layer), (2) Hessian diagonal (approximated via finite differences), (3) Fisher-Rao metric. For each, assign precision tiers and evaluate accuracy. Metrics: (a) correlation between sensitivity metrics and curvature estimates, (b) accuracy on MMLU/GSM8K, (c) computational cost. Expected outcome: curvature-based routing should outperform simpler metrics, justifying the additional spectral estimation cost.

Risk Factors and Limitations: Spectral estimation via power iteration may be sensitive to initialization and convergence criteria; we mitigate via multiple random restarts and explicit convergence thresholds (relative eigenvalue change <1e-4).

Calibration batch size (e.g., 256 tokens) may be insufficient for stable covariance estimation in very deep models; we validate robustness via batch size sweeps (128-1024 tokens) and report confidence intervals.

Condition number thresholds (κ_lo, κ_hi) are hyperparameters requiring tuning; we provide principled bounds via PAC-Bayes theory but acknowledge task-dependent variation. We report sensitivity via ablation studies.

Precision tier assignment is discrete (FP8/INT8/FP16), potentially suboptimal compared to continuous bit allocation; we argue block-level granularity is necessary for deterministic routing and bounded overhead.
Generalization bounds may be loose in practice; we validate empirically via held-out test sets and report tightness ratios, but acknowledge gap between theory and practice.
Curvature estimates assume stationary activation distributions; non-stationary inputs (e.g., out-of-distribution prompts) may violate assumptions. We test robustness via domain shift experiments.
Single-GPU constraint limits calibration batch size; we explicitly quantify how batch size affects eigenvalue approximation quality and provide guidance for practitioners.

Theoretical bounds assume independence of precision errors across layers; in practice, error propagation may compound. We acknowledge this limitation and empirically measure error accumulation via layer-wise ablations.
Empirical Fisher estimation assumes smooth loss landscape; highly non-convex regions may have unstable Hessian estimates. We validate via comparison with finite-difference Hessian approximations.
Framework assumes transformer architecture; generalization to other architectures (CNNs, RNNs) requires separate investigation, which we defer to future work.
